title: Chapter 27: Antibodies
content: Dr. Rose Kim ran her first successful antibody test on Day Twenty-Five.

I knew it was successful because she burst out of the medical bay at 2:00 AM, still wearing her lab coat, her hair escaping its ponytail in every direction, and she grabbed me by the shoulders with a grip that was surprisingly strong for a woman who'd been surviving on vending machine snacks for three weeks.
"It works," she said, her voice cracking. "Her antibodies neutralize the virus in vitro. Luna's blood contains the template for a vaccine."
I was half-asleep, propped up on the couch where I'd been reviewing Mia's patrol reports, and the words took a moment to register.
"A vaccine?"

"Not yet. A *template*. The antibodies are there, they're functional, but they degrade outside her body within hours. I need to map the protein structure, identify the binding sites, and synthesize a stable version. That requires equipment I don't have—centrifuges, PCR machines, a clean room." She released my shoulders and began pacing. "The medical bay is good for trauma care. It's not a virology lab."
"What do you need?"

"A hospital. A real hospital with a functioning bio-containment lab. The CDC regional facility in Portland would be ideal—it's four hundred miles away, which is impossible, but theoretically it has everything I need."
"Four hundred miles through zombie territory. Yeah, that's a suicide march."

"I know. Which is why I'm looking for alternatives." She stopped pacing and turned to face me. "St. Mary's has a virology section in the basement. I couldn't access it before—too many zombies, not enough firepower. But with your group and your weapons..."
"You want to go back."
"Part of me does. The scientist part. The practical part knows it's a long shot." She pushed her glasses up her nose. "But there's something else. Something I didn't tell you."
"I'm listening."

"The virus isn't natural." She said it quietly, like she was afraid the walls might hear. "I've been studying the protein structure since the first samples arrived in Seattle. The mutation rate is too high for a naturally occurring pathogen. The binding mechanism is too efficient. Someone *designed* this virus, Alex. And whoever designed it might have designed a cure."
The implications settled over me like a cold blanket. Engineered virus. Intentional release. A plague that wasn't an accident but a weapon.
"Why would someone release a weapon that kills everyone?"

"That's the question, isn't it?" Rose's eyes were distant, seeing something I couldn't—the inside of her own mind, the place where scientists lived when they were chasing answers that mattered. "Maybe it wasn't supposed to kill everyone. Maybe the virus mutated beyond its intended parameters. Maybe there was supposed to be a vaccine, and something went wrong with the delivery system."
"Or maybe the cure is the system on my wrist."
She looked at my watch. The blue glow was dim in the late-night quiet, but it never fully went dark.

"I've been thinking about that," she admitted. "The system gives you food that reverses zombie degradation. That's not magic—that's biochemistry. Something in the system's food contains a compound that counteracts the virus. If I can isolate that compound..."
"You could skip the hospital entirely."
"In theory. I'd need samples of the system's food, analyzed under controlled conditions. And Luna's continued cooperation for blood draws."

I thought about Luna—the way she flinched at needles, the low growl that escaped her throat when Rose had taken her first blood sample. She cooperated because I asked her to, but it cost her something each time.
"I'll talk to her," I said. "She'll agree. She wants to help."

"She's remarkable," Rose said softly. "Not just the antibody response—her cognitive recovery, her emotional development. She's not curing the virus; she's *transcending* it. If I can understand how..."

Her voice trailed off. The scientist in her was already three steps ahead, sketching experimental protocols and theoretical frameworks, but the woman in her—the one who'd spent three weeks alone in a hospital—was trying to reconcile hope with experience.
"Rose," I said. "You don't have to do this alone anymore."

She looked at me, and for a moment, the clinical mask slipped entirely. Underneath was someone who was tired, and scared, and desperately clinging to purpose because purpose was the only thing keeping her upright.
"I know," she said. "That's the hardest part."
I talked to Luna the next morning.

She was in the hydroponic garden bay, tending the lettuce with the careful, methodical attention she brought to everything that grew. Her hands were gentle with the plants—a stark contrast to the violence those same hands could inflict—and she hummed while she worked, a tuneless melody that was becoming more musical every day.
"Rose needs more blood," I said.
"I know." She didn't look up from the lettuce. "Can hear her thinking about it. She wants everything. All my blood. Every day."
"Not every day. And not all of it. She just needs regular samples to track the antibody levels."
"For the cure."
"For the cure."

Luna plucked a leaf and held it up to the light, examining its edges with the curiosity of someone who was still learning what the world looked like. She'd never seen a lettuce plant before—not that she remembered, anyway—and the novelty of growing things hadn't worn off.
"The cure would help people," she said. "Not just me. Everyone who turned. Everyone who died."
"It could. If Rose can make it work."
"Then she can have my blood." Luna set down the lettuce and turned to face me. "But I want something in return."
"Name it."

"When the cure works—*if* it works—I want to be the first one. I want to be fully human again." Her voice was steady, but her eyes were fierce. "I am tired of being half-dead, Alex. I am tired of being cold. I am tired of my body wanting to eat people when my mind wants to eat pasta."
"You eat the raw beef because—"

"I eat the raw beef because the system says it helps. But I *want* pasta. I want to taste things the way you taste them. I want my heart to beat because it chooses to, not because a virus is keeping it going." She stepped closer, her dark eyes locked on mine. "I want to be a person. Not a zombie. Not a half-thing. A person. Can the cure do that?"
"I don't know. But Rose is going to try."

"Then I will give her everything she needs." Luna's hand found mine—warm now, fully warm, her grip strong and certain. "Because I believe in trying. Because you taught me that trying is what separates the living from the dead."
"Trying and staying," I said.
"Trying, staying, and together." She squeezed my hand. "Three words. Enough to live by."
**AFFECTION UPDATE: LUNA — 68%**
The number climbed, and this time I felt it—not as a system metric, but as a truth I could hold in my hand, in the warmth of Luna's fingers and the conviction in her voice.
She was fighting her way back. Not just from death, but toward life—real life, human life, a future that wasn't defined by what the virus had taken.
And I was going to help her get there, system rewards be damned.
Some things mattered more than numbers.
Luna was one of them.
